stem: Using integration by parts with \( u = [[c]]x \) and \( dv = e^{-[[k]]x}\,dx \), evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_{0}^{[[m]]} [[c]]x\,e^{-[[k]]x}\,dx \).
solution: With \( u=[[c]]x,\ dv=e^{-[[k]]x}dx \) so \( du=[[c]]\,dx,\ v=-\tfrac{1}{[[k]]}e^{-[[k]]x} \): \( \int [[c]]x e^{-[[k]]x}dx = -\tfrac{[[c]]x}{[[k]]}e^{-[[k]]x} - \displaystyle\int -\tfrac{[[c]]}{[[k]]}e^{-[[k]]x}dx = -\tfrac{[[c]]x}{[[k]]}e^{-[[k]]x} - \tfrac{[[c]]}{[[k]]^{2}}e^{-[[k]]x} \). Evaluating from \( 0 \) to \( [[m]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly assumes the boundary contribution at \( x=[[m]] \) vanishes entirely (treating it like an improper integral at infinity).
This keeps only the boundary term from parts and drops the remaining exponential integral.
This uses the antiderivative coefficient alone with no boundary evaluation at all.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.

stem: Applying integration by parts TWICE (a reduction in the power of \( x \) each time), evaluate the improper integral \( \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} [[c]]x^{2}e^{-[[k]]x}\,dx \).
solution: First parts step (\( u=[[c]]x^{2} \), \( dv=e^{-[[k]]x}dx \)) reduces the power of \( x \) by one and leaves \( \tfrac{2\cdot[[c]]}{[[k]]}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty}xe^{-[[k]]x}dx \); a second parts step (or the known result \( \int_0^\infty xe^{-kx}dx=1/k^2 \)) finishes it. All boundary terms vanish as \( x\to\infty \) since the exponential decays faster than any power of \( x \) grows. The result is \( \dfrac{2\cdot[[c]]}{[[k]]^{3}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of 2 that appears after two applications of parts (\( n! \) for the reduction of \( x^{2} \)).
This uses the wrong power of \( [[k]] \) in the denominator, as if only one parts step had been applied to \( x^2 \) directly.
This is the result for \( \int_0^\infty [[c]]xe^{-[[k]]x}dx \) (only one power of \( x \)), not \( x^2 \).
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Applying integration by parts TWICE and solving algebraically for the original integral (it reappears on both sides), evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\pi/[[b]]} [[c]]e^{[[a]]x}\sin([[b]]x)\,dx \).
solution: The cyclic identity \( \int e^{ax}\sin(bx)\,dx = \dfrac{e^{ax}\left(a\sin(bx)-b\cos(bx)\right)}{a^{2}+b^{2}} \) comes from applying parts twice and solving for the integral once it reappears. Substituting \( a=[[a]],\,b=[[b]],\,c=[[c]] \) and evaluating from \( 0 \) to \( \pi/[[b]] \) (where \( \sin([[b]]x) \) vanishes at both ends but \( \cos([[b]]x) \) does not) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the exponential growth factor entirely, as if the boundary term at the upper limit did not contribute.
This swaps the roles of \( a \) and \( b \) in the cyclic formula's numerator.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Using integration by parts with \( u = [[c]]x \) and \( dv = \sec^{2}x\,dx \), evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\pi/[[n]]} [[c]]x\sec^{2}x\,dx \).
solution: With \( u=[[c]]x,\ dv=\sec^{2}x\,dx,\ v=\tan x \): \( \int [[c]]x\sec^{2}x\,dx = [[c]]x\tan x - [[c]]\displaystyle\int \tan x\,dx = [[c]]x\tan x + [[c]]\ln|\cos x| \). Evaluating from \( 0 \) to \( \pi/[[n]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the boundary term \( [[c]]x\tan x \) and forgets the logarithmic correction from \( -\int\tan x\,dx \).
This keeps only the logarithmic correction term and drops the \( x\tan x \) boundary contribution.
This flips the sign of both terms in the antiderivative.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Using the TABULAR method (repeated integration by parts on \( u=[[c]]x^{2} \) until it reaches zero), evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\pi} [[c]]x^{2}\sin([[k]]x)\,dx \).
solution: The tabular pattern (alternating signs, differentiating \( x^{2}\to 2x\to 2\to 0 \) and antidifferentiating \( \sin([[k]]x) \) each row) gives \( \int x^{2}\sin(kx)\,dx = -\tfrac{x^{2}}{k}\cos(kx) + \tfrac{2x}{k^{2}}\sin(kx) + \tfrac{2}{k^{3}}\cos(kx) \). Scaling by \( [[c]] \) and evaluating from \( 0 \) to \( \pi \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the leading \( -\tfrac{x^2}{k}\cos(kx) \) row of the tabular pattern and drops the remaining two rows.
This keeps the leading row but with the wrong sign.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
